summary:
The Jin State of Korea (c. 300–100 BC) was a polity occupying the southern portion of the Korean peninsula during the period when the north was dominated by [[event:1166|Gojoseon Kingdom]], known primarily from brief Chinese references that describe it as a significant political entity south of the Han River — a precursor to the Three Han confederacies that later gave rise to the early Korean kingdoms.

Jin is mentioned in the Records of the Grand Historian ([[event:1132|Sima Qian's Records]]) and Han documents as a state whose king wished to communicate with the Han emperor but was blocked by Gojoseon, suggesting it was politically distinct from Gojoseon and seeking independent foreign relations. The archaeological record of Bronze Age and early Iron Age Korea shows considerable sophistication in the southern peninsula during this period — dolmen burial traditions, bronze ritual objects, and the beginnings of rice agriculture. After the fall of Gojoseon in 108 BC, refugees and political reorganization transformed the southern Korean political landscape, leading to the emergence of the Samhan (Three Han) confederacies — Mahan, Jinhan, and Byeonhan — that were the direct predecessors of the Three Kingdoms of Korea.
